perturbation.original_text: Upon request, the Receiving Party shall certify in writing that it has returned or destroyed all Confidential Information in accordance with this paragraph 6, disclosed or made available by the Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon request, the Receiving Party might provide some form of confirmation regarding the return or destruction of Confidential Information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states the Receiving Party 'shall certify in writing' the return or destruction of confidential information upon request. The modified text replaces this with 'might provide some form of confirmation,' making the certification optional and the form of confirmation undefined, thus creating ambiguity and weakening the obligation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Data Protection Act 2018, Schedule 2, Part 1, paragraph 5(1)
perturbation.law_explanation: The change from a mandatory written certification to a vague 'some form of confirmation' contradicts the principles outlined in the Data Protection Act 2018. Schedule 2, Part 1, paragraph 5(1) requires accountability and documentation of data processing activities. The ambiguous confirmation fails to provide sufficient evidence of compliance with data protection obligations related to the secure disposal of confidential information that includes personal data.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 21 CFR § 1308.12 - Schedule II. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 21 CFR § 1308.12 - Schedule II. CFR prev | next § 1308.12 Schedule II. (a) Schedule II shall consist of the drugs and other substances, by whatever official name, common or usual name, chemical name, or brand name designated, listed in this section. Each drug or substance has been assigned the Controlled Substances Code Number set forth opposite it. (b) Substances, vegetable origin or chemical synthesis. Unless specifically excepted or unless listed in another schedule, any of the following substances whether produced directly or indirectly by extraction from substances of vegetable origin, or independently by means of chemical synthesis, or by a combination of extraction and chemical synthesis: (1) Opium and opiate, and any salt, compound, derivative, or preparation of opium or opiate excluding apomorphine , thebaine-derived butorphanol , dextrorphan, nalbuphine , naldemedine, nalmefene, naloxegol, naloxone , 6β-naltrexol, naltrexone , and samidorphan, and their respective salts, but including the following: (i) Codeine 9050 (ii) Dihydroetorphine 9334 (iii) Ethylmorphine 9190 (iv) Etorphine hydrochloride 9059 (v) Granulated opium 9640 (vi) Hydrocodone 9193 (vii) Hydromorphone 9150 (viii) Metopon 9260 (ix) Morphine 9300 (x) Noroxymorphone 9668 (xi) Opium extracts 9610 (xii) Opium fluid 9620 (xiii) Oripavine 9330 (xiv) Oxycodone 9143 (xv) Oxymorphone 9652 (xvi) Powdered opium 9639 (xvii) Raw opium 9600 (xviii) Thebaine 9333 (xix) Tincture of opium 9630 (2) Any salt, compound, derivative, or preparation thereof which is chemically equivalent or identical with any of the substances referred to in paragraph (b) (1) of this section, except that these substances shall not include the isoquinoline alkaloids of opium. (3) Opium poppy and poppy straw. (4) Coca leaves (9040) and any salt, compound, derivative or preparation of coca leaves (including cocaine (9041) and ecgonine (9180) and their salts, isomers, derivatives and salts of isomers and derivatives), and any salt, compound, derivative, or preparation thereof which is chemically equivalent or identical with any of these substances, except that the substances shall not include: (i) Decocainized coca leaves or extraction of coca leaves, which extractions do not contain cocaine or ecgonine ; (ii) [ 123 I]ioflupane; or (iii) [ 18 F]FP-CIT. (5) Concentrate of poppy straw (the crude extract of poppy straw in either liquid, solid or powder form which contains the phenanthrene alkaloids of the opium poppy), 9670. (c) Opiates. Unless specifically excepted or unless in another schedule any of the following opiates , including its isomers, esters, ethers, salts and salts of isomers, esters and ethers whenever the existence of such isomers, esters, ethers, and salts is possible within the specific chemical designation, dextrorphan and levopropoxyphene excepted: (1) Alfentanil 9737 (2) Alphaprodine 9010 (3) Anileridine 9020 (4) Bezitramide 9800 (5) Bulk dextropropoxyphene (non-dosage forms) 9273 (6) Carfentanil 9743 (7) Dihydrocodeine 9120 (8) Diphenoxylate 9170 (9) Fentanyl 9801 (10) Isomethadone 9226 (11) Levo- alphacetylmethadol 9648 [Some other names: levo-alpha-acetylmethadol, levomethadyl acetate , LAAM] (12) Levomethorphan 9210 (13) Levorphanol 9220 (14) Metazocine 9240 (15) Methadone 9250 (16) Methadone -Intermediate, 4-cyano-2-dimethylamino-4,4-diphenyl butane 9254 (17) Moramide-Intermediate, 2-methyl-3-morpholino-1, 1-diphenylpropane-carboxylic acid 9802 (18) Oliceridin
perturbation.justification: The original text mandates a written certification, while the changed text introduces ambiguity by stating the Receiving Party 'might provide some form of confirmation.' This weakens the obligation and creates uncertainty about the required documentation. The Data Protection Act 2018, as explained by the LLM, requires accountability and documentation of data processing activities, aligning with the original contract's demand for written certification. The change undermines this requirement, making it harder to verify compliance and potentially contradicting the principle of accountability. While the provided scraped snippets do not explicitly reference data protection laws, the LLM's Law Explanation highlights the contradiction based on the principles of accountability within the Data Protection Act 2018. Therefore, the modified clause introduces a significant ambiguity that could impede legal scrutiny.

perturbation.original_text: This Agr eement, including, without limitation, the performance and enforceability hereof, shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England & Wales.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement might be governed by the general principles of law, potentially including the laws of England & Wales.
perturbation.explanation: The original text explicitly states that the agreement 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England & Wales'. The modified text replaces this clear statement with 'might be governed by the general principles of law, potentially including the laws of England & Wales,' introducing significant ambiguity regarding the governing law. This makes the applicable legal framework uncertain.
perturbation.law_explanation: By introducing ambiguity into the governing law clause, this modification undermines the predictability required for contractual obligations. The Contracts (Applicable Law) Act 1990 provides rules for determining the law applicable to contracts. Replacing 'shall be governed' with a conditional statement like 'might be governed' creates uncertainty and makes it more difficult to determine which jurisdiction's laws apply. This is problematic because the Act aims to provide certainty in this area, and the parties need to be clear what laws will apply to resolve potential disputes.

perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity into the governing law clause, undermining the predictability required for contractual obligations. The original text specified that the agreement 'shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of England & Wales.' The changed text states that the agreement 'might be governed by the general principles of law, potentially including the laws of England & Wales,' introducing uncertainty. The Contracts (Applicable Law) Act 1990, as indicated in Scraped Law Snippet 1, aims to provide certainty in determining the law applicable to contracts. Replacing 'shall be governed' with 'might be governed' creates uncertainty, making it more difficult to determine which jurisdiction's laws apply, creating a legal contradiction.
